provenance.source_corpus: Substrate comparison for short-term success of a multispecies tree plantation in thickened tailings of a boreal gold mine

source_spans.text: compost addition to tailings increased survival through substrate structure improvement.
compost addition to tailings increased survival through substrate structure improvement.
compost addition to tailings increased survival through substrate structure improvement.
Compost added to tailings improved the structure of the mixture by increasing the macroporosity and decreasing the bulk density
Compost added to tailings improved the structure of the mixture by increasing the macroporosity and decreasing the bulk density
Trees did not survive the first winter when planted directly in tailings

Tree growth was influenced by topsoil thickness (50 vs. 20 cm) rather than volume (20 cm layer vs. 20 cm deep planting holes); trees grown in the thicker topsoil showed the highest N nutrition and aerial growth.

Tree growth was influenced by topsoil thickness (50 vs. 20 cm) rather than volume (20 cm layer vs. 20 cm deep planting holes); trees grown in the thicker topsoil showed the highest N nutrition and aerial growth.

Tree growth was influenced by topsoil thickness (50 vs. 20 cm) rather than volume (20 cm layer vs. 20 cm deep planting holes); trees grown in the thicker topsoil showed the highest N nutrition and aerial growth.

nodes.name: Adding compost to tailings or mineral soil

source_spans.text: Adding compost to tailings or mineral soil improved the P nutrition of trees
Adding compost to tailings or mineral soil improved the P nutrition of trees
substrate macroporosity appeared to be positively and linearly correlated to poplar above ground biomass
Nitrogen concentrations were positively and linearly correlated with all aboveground growth parameters of poplar
Nitrogen concentrations were positively and linearly correlated with all aboveground growth parameters of poplar

nodes.name: neutral pH of the mine tailings

source_spans.text: trace metal accumulations in all tree species were low, probably due to the neutral pH of the mine tailings
